title: TLS, SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, Bucket Keys, and Encryption Boundaries · Execution Trace: Kms Key Policy Context
summary: A service labels default encryption end-to-end, grants a broad KMS key policy, logs encryption context, and cannot rotate or revoke access without breaking unknown consumers. Predict the representation, storage, trust, cost, and recovery evidence before inspecting a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the kms key policy context increment in encryption and key-authorization contract. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence…
objectives: Constrain IAM and KMS key policy, grants, service principal, encryption context, region, rotation, disable, deletion, and break-glass ownership.
Choose SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, dual-layer KMS where required, or client encryption from audit, tenancy, performance, cost, and service integration needs.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Predict Kms Key Policy Context · Theory Kms Key Policy Context
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go file-delivery team handles case 2237 during a private media release. Build deterministic Go evidence to separate transport, storage, KMS authorization, context, and rotation evidence; keep browser execution deterministic and move AWS claims to an authorized transfer gate; then cancel halfway through and prove every body, part, and goroutine reaches a terminal state. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

checks.description: The evidence note explains the model, failure risk, and verification in the learner’s own words.
checks.failureMessage: Add a causal explanation using constrain and policy plus specific case evidence.
